skill.name: llm-app-patterns
skill.description: Production-ready patterns for building LLM applications. Covers RAG pipelines, agent architectures, prompt IDEs, and LLMOps monitoring.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide to LLM application development patterns including RAG, agents, prompt engineering, and production operations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation file containing educational content about LLM application patterns. All static analysis findings are false positives caused by markdown formatting. The backticks flagged are code block delimiters and ASCII art borders, not shell command execution. URLs are documentation references, not active network calls. Code examples like hashlib.sha256 are illustrative and use secure algorithms. No executable code or security risks detected.

content.value_statement: Building LLM applications requires navigating complex architectural decisions. This skill provides battle-tested patterns for RAG pipelines, agent systems, and production operations.
actual_capabilities: Design RAG pipelines with chunking, embedding, and retrieval strategies
Implement agent architectures including ReAct, function calling, and multi-agent systems
Create prompt templates with versioning and A/B testing
Set up LLMOps monitoring for latency, quality, and cost metrics
Apply production patterns like caching, rate limiting, and fallbacks
limitations: Code examples are illustrative and require adaptation to specific frameworks
Does not provide ready-to-use library implementations
Vector database choices require external setup and configuration
LLM API integration requires valid API keys and accounts

use_cases.title: RAG Knowledge Base
use_cases.description: Build a question-answering system grounded in your documentation using hybrid search and contextual compression.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building AI-powered support tools

use_cases.title: Agent Task Automation
use_cases.description: Create multi-step agents that can search, calculate, and synthesize information using the ReAct or Plan-and-Execute patterns.
use_cases.target_user: AI engineers developing autonomous workflow automation

use_cases.title: LLM Production Monitoring
use_cases.description: Implement observability for LLM applications with metrics tracking, distributed tracing, and evaluation frameworks.
use_cases.target_user: ML Ops engineers managing production AI systems

prompt_templates.title: Basic RAG Prompt
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for simple retrieval-augmented generation when you need to ground responses in provided documents

prompt_templates.title: ReAct Agent Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt:
You are an AI assistant that can use tools to answer questions.

Available tools:
{tools_description}

Use this format:
Thought: [your reasoning about what to do next]
Action: [tool_name(arguments)]
Observation: [tool result]
... (repeat as needed)
Thought: I have enough information to answer
Final Answer: [your response]

Question: {question}
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when building agents that need to reason through multi-step problems using external tools

prompt_templates.title: Few-Shot Prompt with Examples
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you need consistent output format or behavior by demonstrating examples

prompt_templates.title: Prompt Chain for Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for complex tasks requiring multiple LLM calls with intermediate processing steps

output_examples.input: User asks: What is the company's refund policy?
output: Retrieves relevant policy documents from vector database
Generates answer grounded in retrieved context with source citations
Returns response with confidence score and document references

output_examples.input: User asks: Plan a research project on climate change impacts
output: Creates plan with steps: gather data, analyze trends, identify sources, draft report
Executes each step sequentially with tool calls
Synthesizes findings into comprehensive research outline

best_practices: Use hybrid search combining semantic and keyword matching for better retrieval accuracy
Implement caching for deterministic prompts to reduce latency and costs
Track key metrics like latency, token usage, and user satisfaction for continuous improvement
anti_patterns: Using fixed-size chunking without considering document structure, which breaks context
Skipping evaluation and monitoring, making it impossible to detect quality degradation
Not implementing fallback strategies when primary LLM providers experience outages

faq.question: What is the difference between RAG and fine-tuning?
faq.answer: RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and provides them as context, allowing the model to access up-to-date information without retraining. Fine-tuning adjusts model weights on training data, which is better for learning style or format but cannot add new knowledge after training.

faq.question: How do I choose between different agent architectures?
faq.answer: Use ReAct for interactive multi-step reasoning with tool use. Use function calling for structured API integrations. Use Plan-and-Execute for complex tasks requiring upfront planning. Use multi-agent systems when specialized expertise is needed for different subtasks.

faq.question: What vector database should I use for production?
faq.answer: For managed service with high scale, use Pinecone. For self-hosted with GraphQL, use Weaviate. For existing PostgreSQL infrastructure, use pgvector. For prototyping, use ChromaDB for simplicity.

faq.question: How can I reduce LLM API costs?
faq.answer: Implement semantic caching for similar queries, use smaller models for simple tasks, optimize prompt length, set appropriate temperature (0 for cacheable responses), and use fallback models when primary is rate-limited.

faq.question: What metrics should I track for LLM applications?
faq.answer: Track performance metrics (latency, tokens per second), quality metrics (user satisfaction, task completion rate), cost metrics (cost per request, cache hit rate), and reliability metrics (error rate, timeout rate).

faq.question: How do I handle hallucinations in RAG systems?
faq.answer: Instruct the model to say 'I don't know' when context is insufficient, use groundedness scoring in evaluation, implement retrieval quality checks, and consider adding a verification step where the model cites sources for claims.
